ops.body:
# env sync reconciliation design

Deliberate safety/consistency choices in the `.env` sync feature (internal/env + the reconcile env pass + rpc-serve's `env.get_state`). Changing any of these without understanding the tradeoff reintroduces a deadlock, a divergence loop, or a secret leak.

## Lock-free serving (deadlock)

`env.get_state` (internal/cli/rpcserve.go) is strictly read-only and takes NO flock. Every host's converge pass holds its own flock **across** peer fetches (synckit converge.Reconcile does the same for the repo registry), so a locking handler deadlocks the mesh the moment two hosts converge at once. Serving is safe lock-free because `env.Observe` is idempotent: stamps derive from file mtimes, so the same file state + sidecar always folds to identical output with zero writes.

## Invariant I1 (the sidecar is the merge base)

A persisted sidecar's present-key set always equals the file's KV content at persist time. There is no separate base snapshot — the sidecar doubles as the 3-way base, which is why:
- a busy pass (quiet window) must persist **nothing**; `env.ApplyAll` enforces this as a two-pass all-or-nothing gate — plan and freshly Lstat every file that would change first, write only when none was modified within `QuietWindow`;
- `dropUnsyncable` purges tracked/exempt/invalid names from the merged state before persist;
- `Apply`/`ApplyAll` and `Observe` share the same Lstat-based `Exempt` predicate (symlinks, non-regular, oversized) so neither ever reads through or writes over a path the other refuses.

Accepted crash-consistency limits (documented in the package doc, deliberately not journaled away): a crash or write error between applying files and persisting the sidecar re-stamps just-imported values at apply time on the next pass (self-corrects on the key's next edit); a `git add` or symlink swap racing the milliseconds between the final checks and the rename is overwritten once (git's index copy survives; the next pass purges the file).

## Origin peer asymmetry (propagation)

The env fetch loop must NOT skip the notifying `origin` peer — the opposite of the repo-registry converge. For repos, content flows via the git origin server; for env, the notifying host is the only holder of the new content. Termination comes from the apply-stable digest (values-only, no stamps/tombstones/layout), not the origin skip. Pinned by `TestConvergeEnvFetchesOriginPeer`.

## Whole-payload rejection (poisoning)

`validatePeerEnv` rejects a peer's ENTIRE payload on any wire violation: unrequested origin, bad file name (`ValidateFileName`: bare `.env`/`.env.*`, ≤255 bytes, no control chars), invalid key, value containing a newline (would break the re-parse invariant and set up a divergence loop), stamp outside `[0, now+MaxStampSkew]` (a MaxInt64 stamp would permanently win LWW and overflow `stampFor`'s floor+1 bump), or wire caps (`MaxWireFiles`/`MaxWireKeys`/`AggregateSize ≤ MaxFileSize`, where `AggregateSize` counts key+value+2 separator bytes per entry — the same formula the merge cap uses). Partial application of a malicious payload is never acceptable; every host must make identical decisions or the mesh diverges. Per-peer fetches are batched at `env.MaxOrigins` (the handler's request cap); any batch failure drops the whole peer for the pass — no partial trust.

## Caps closed under merge (propagation break)

Individually valid peer payloads can merge into a state exceeding the wire caps; a host persisting that state would be rejected whole by every peer thereafter — a permanent propagation break. `capMergedToWire` (inside `dropUnsyncable`, the choke point both the serve and converge paths share) drops over-cap files and keeps only the lexicographically-smallest `MaxWireFiles` names, deterministically from the converged state, so all hosts drop identically and no host ever holds a state it cannot serve. Pinned by `TestConvergeEnvCapsMergedFiles`.

## Eligibility reloaded under the flock

`convergeEnvWith` recomputes the eligible-repo set from a freshly reloaded state registry AFTER acquiring the flock (reload seam; production passes `state.Load`), so a `repo rm` or `NoEnvSync` opt-out landing during the unlocked peer-fetch window is never applied to. `dl`/`configDir` stay from the pre-pass state. Pinned by `TestConvergeEnvEligibilityReloadedUnderLock`.

## Asymmetric tombstone persistence (spam vs deletion)

`dropTombstoneOnlyAbsent` prunes a merged name only when it has no present keys AND no local regular file AND is absent from the loaded base sidecar. Peer-injected tombstone spam under never-local names therefore never persists, while a locally deleted file's name stays in the sidecar (with blank-valued tombstones) and keeps propagating its deletion until `TombstoneTTL` GC empties it. Narrowing or widening this condition either resurrects deleted files from peers or reopens unbounded sidecar growth. Pinned by `TestConvergeEnvWholeFileDeletionPropagates` and `TestConvergeEnvDropsNeverLocalTombstone`.

## Shared LocalEnvState filtering (leaks)

`reconcile.LocalEnvState` is the ONE place that computes scan → sidecar → Observe → drop(tracked/exempt/invalid). Both the rpc handler and the converge pass call it; duplicating the logic lets the two paths drift, and the drift is a leak (e.g. a file that became git-tracked after being synced would keep being served — Observe re-unions sidecar names past the scan filter by design). Tombstone values are blanked on Remove and at sidecar Save so deleted secrets never linger on the wire or disk for the GC window.
